explanation: The phrase "Once prop_0, prop_1" in the context of a general system property (reinforced by "This property holds for every DMi") indicates that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 also holds at that same point in time. This translates to G(prop_0 -> prop_1). The subsequent phrase "In this way prop_2" implies that prop_2 also holds under the same conditions or as a concurrent consequence when prop_0 is true. Therefore, if prop_0 holds, both prop_1 and prop_2 must hold simultaneously. Combining these, we get G(prop_0 -> (prop_1 & prop_2)). The final sentence "This property holds for every DMi" confirms the global (G) nature of the statement.

The phrase "Once prop_0, prop_1" means that at some point in the future, both `prop_0` and `prop_1` will be true simultaneously. This is captured by `F (prop_0 & prop_1)`. The follow-up phrase "In this way prop_2" implies that `prop_2` is also true at that same moment or as a direct, simultaneous consequence of `prop_0` and `prop_1` occurring. Combining these, the most direct interpretation is that all three propositions are true together at some future point. The final phrase "This property holds for every DMi" indicates the scope of the property but does not translate into an LTL operator or additional atomic propositions, as LTL describes a single system trace and we are limited to the provided propositions.

The phrase "Once prop_0, prop_1" typically means that at some future point in time, prop_0 and prop_1 will both be true. This translates to F(prop_0 & prop_1). The subsequent phrase "In this way prop_2" indicates that prop_2 is also true at that same point in time, describing the conditions or manner of the preceding event. Therefore, all three propositions (prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2) are asserted to be true simultaneously at some future moment. The final part, "This property holds for every DMi," indicates the universal applicability of the LTL statement itself and does not introduce new LTL operators or atomic propositions.

The phrase "Once prop_0, prop_1" suggests an implication: whenever 'prop_0' occurs, 'prop_1' also occurs at that time. This is best represented as 'prop_0 -> prop_1'. The subsequent phrase "In this way prop_2" indicates that 'prop_2' is true as a consequence or alongside the condition described. This implies that when 'prop_0' occurs, not only 'prop_1' but also 'prop_2' is true. Therefore, the combined immediate implication is 'prop_0 -> (prop_1 & prop_2)'. Finally, "This property holds for every DMi" means this condition is universally true throughout time, which translates to the 'G' (globally) operator applied to the entire implication.

The phrase "Once prop_0, prop_1" is interpreted as a general rule: whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 also occurs. This translates to G (prop_0 -> prop_1). The subsequent phrase "In this way prop_2" implies that prop_2 is a characteristic or consequence that also holds under the same conditions described by the first part. Therefore, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 AND prop_2 must be true. Combining these, the overall meaning is that globally, if prop_0 is true, then both prop_1 and prop_2 are true. The last sentence, "This property holds for every DMi," describes the scope of the property but does not translate into an additional LTL operator.

The phrase "Once DUi prop_0, this component must prop_1" means that eventually, prop_0 will occur, and from that point onwards, prop_1 must continuously hold true. This is best captured by the LTL formula F (prop_0 & G prop_1). The phrase "In this way the prop_2" implies that prop_2 is a general and continuous property of the system, meaning it must always hold. This translates to G prop_2. Since both conditions must hold, they are combined with an AND operator.

The phrase "Once DUi prop_0" indicates that `prop_0` eventually becomes true (captured by the `F` operator and `prop_0`). The phrase "this component must prop_1" signifies that from the moment `prop_0` becomes true, `prop_1` must hold indefinitely (captured by `G prop_1`). The combination "Once DUi prop_0, this component must prop_1" therefore translates to `F (prop_0 & G prop_1)`. The subsequent phrase "In this way the user prop_2" implies that `prop_2` is true at the very moment `prop_0` triggers the perpetual truth of `prop_1`. This is added as a conjunction (`& prop_2`) at that specific point in time. The last sentence "This property holds for every DMi" simply states that the property applies generally and does not introduce additional LTL operators, as LTL formulas describe general properties of a system's execution.

The phrase "Once prop_0, prop_1" is interpreted as a universal implication: "Always, if prop_0 becomes true, then prop_1 eventually becomes true." The phrase "In this way prop_2" specifies the manner in which prop_1 follows prop_0, indicating that prop_2 must hold during this process. The LTL 'until' operator (U) is suitable for capturing this. Specifically, 'prop_2 U prop_1' means 'prop_2' is true until 'prop_1' becomes true, and 'prop_1' must eventually become true. Therefore, the complete statement translates to: 'Always, if prop_0 is true, then prop_2 holds until prop_1 becomes true'.

The phrase "Once prop_0" indicates that if `prop_0` ever becomes true, a certain condition must follow. This is best captured by a global implication, `G(prop_0 -> ...)`. The phrase "this component must prop_1" signifies an eventual requirement for `prop_1` to become true, which translates to `F prop_1`. The phrase "In this way prop_2" modifies the previous statement, implying that `prop_2` must also hold when `prop_1` occurs. Therefore, instead of just `F prop_1`, it becomes `F (prop_1 & prop_2)`. Combining these, the LTL statement means: Globally, if `prop_0` becomes true, then eventually `prop_1` and `prop_2` must both be true.

The phrase "Once prop_0, prop_1" is interpreted as a general rule, meaning "Whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 is also true." This translates to `G(prop_0 -> prop_1)`. The subsequent phrase "In this way prop_2" indicates that `prop_2` also holds under the same conditions that link `prop_0` and `prop_1`. Therefore, when `prop_0` is true, both `prop_1` and `prop_2` must be true simultaneously. This combines into `G(prop_0 -> (prop_1 & prop_2))`. The final part, "This property holds for every DMi,", describes the scope of the property and does not translate into LTL operators.
